answer:
**In the Old English poem "Widsith" (chapter 14 of *Old English Elegies*), the narrator Widsith, a travelling scop, claims to have visited numerous kings and their courts across tribes and nations, reciting songs in exchange for treasure.[^1] Three specific kings he names as hosts are:**

- **Eormanric**, king of the Goths, who treated him well and gave him a valuable gold ring;[^2]
- **Guthere**, king of the Burgundians, who rewarded his song with a gleaming treasure;[^3]
- **Ælfwine**, son of Eadwine, whom he visited in Italy and praises for generosity with rings.[^4]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14 ["Widsith matholade, wordhord onleac,"](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/).

[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14, [p-f8162a](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-f8162a).

[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14, [p-8276c0](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-8276c0).

[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14, [p-8276c0](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-8276c0).
